---
title: "Session 3: AI Meets Your Data"
subtitle: "Business Intelligence & AI"
author: "Kerry Back, Rice University"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, revealjs-style.scss]
    slide-number: c
    transition: fade
    navigation-mode: linear
    width: 1920
    height: 1080
    margin: 0.05
    center: true
---

## From Personal Productivity to Enterprise Data {.section-divider}

::: {.notes}
This section divider opens the bridge segment (minutes 0-8). You are transitioning the class from "personal AI" mode --- where they spent a week generating charts, documents, and workflows with Claude --- into "enterprise AI" mode, where the same technology connects to corporate databases and company documents. Let the title land, then advance.
:::

---

## The Shift

::: {.explainer}
You have spent a week using AI for **personal productivity** --- charts, documents, workflows. Now we scale up. The same AI that drafts your memos can also **query your company's databases** and **search your company's documents**.
:::

::: {.highlight-box}
But enterprise data is scattered across 5, 10, sometimes 20 systems --- and trapped in policies, contracts, and board minutes nobody can search. Today we see what that looks like and what AI can do about it.
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "You have spent a week using AI for personal productivity --- charts, documents, workflows. You have iterated, critiqued, and built reusable prompts. Now we scale up. The same AI that drafts your memos can also query your company's databases and search your company's documents."

"By the end of this session, you will have queried enterprise databases in plain English, watched AI merge data across systems that don't talk to each other, seen AI answer questions from company documents with page-number citations, and started mapping your own company's data landscape. Let's jump right in."

Transition: advance to the cold open.
:::

---

## The 3-Week Email Chain {.section-divider}

::: {.notes}
This is the cold open provocation (minutes 8-13). The goal is to create an emotional anchor: the frustration of waiting weeks for a simple cross-system question. You will present the scenario and then immediately contrast it with the XYZ Corp Data Simulation demo.
:::

---

## "Which of Our Customers Buy from Multiple Divisions?"

::: {.two-cards}
::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
The CEO's Question
:::
- Simple enough to ask in one sentence
- Requires data from 3 separate CRMs
- Nobody owns the cross-system view
- The answer should drive pricing strategy
:::

::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
The 3-Week Reality
:::
- Week 1: BI team extracts from Salesforce
- Week 2: Energy team pulls from legacy CRM
- Week 3: Manual matching in Excel, finally delivered
- [CEO has moved on to a different priority]{.accent}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "Here is a scenario that might feel familiar. A CEO asks a simple question: which of our customers buy from multiple divisions? It is one sentence. It should be answerable. But here is what actually happens."

Walk through the right card: "Week one, the BI team extracts data from Salesforce. Week two, someone from the Energy division pulls from their legacy CRM. Week three, an analyst is manually matching customer names in Excel. By the time the answer arrives, the CEO has moved on to a different priority."

Ask the room: "Has this happened to you? A simple business question that took weeks because the data lived in different systems? Raise your hand if the answer is yes."

Pause for responses. Then: "What I am about to show you takes about 30 seconds."
:::

---

## Demo: Dashboard vs. Chat {.section-divider}

::: {.notes}
This opens the live demo segment (minutes 13-23). Make sure the XYZ Corp Data Simulation is open in another browser tab and ready. You are about to ask the same cross-system question and get an instant answer. This is the "wow" moment of the session.
:::

---

## Introducing XYZ Corp

::: {.info-box}
::: {.box-title}
The Simulation Environment
:::
- **$500M B2B industrial distributor** with 3 operating divisions
- **9 enterprise systems**: Salesforce, Legacy CRM, HubSpot, NetSuite, SAP, Oracle SCM, Workday, Zendesk, QuickBooks
- **41 tables, 26,000+ rows, 3 years of data**
- Realistic cross-system friction: different naming conventions, date formats, column styles
- **Plus a document collection**: employee handbook, vendor contracts, board minutes, strategic plans
:::

::: {.explainer}
You will log in and query this environment yourself in 15 minutes. First, watch what happens when we ask it the CEO's question.
:::

::: {.notes}
Briefly introduce the XYZ Corp Data Simulation. Say: "This is the environment you will be working with for the rest of the course. XYZ Corp is a fictional $500M B2B industrial supplies distributor. It has three divisions --- Industrial, Energy, and Safety --- each with their own CRM, plus shared finance, HR, and support systems. Nine systems total. Forty-one database tables. Three years of realistic data."

"It also has an internal document collection --- an employee handbook, vendor contracts, board minutes, and strategic plans. These are the kinds of unstructured documents that trap knowledge in every organization. You will query both databases and documents today."

"The key word is realistic. The systems have different naming conventions. The Legacy CRM stores dates as text strings. Customers appear under different names across divisions --- General Electric in one system, GE Industrial Solutions in another. This is what real enterprise data looks like."

Switch to the XYZ Corp Data Simulation and prepare for the live demo.
:::

---

## Live Demo: "Which Customers Buy from Multiple Divisions?" {.shrink}

::: {.code-box}
::: {.box-title}
The prompt
:::
```
Which of our customers buy from multiple divisions?
Show combined revenue and flag any name mismatches across systems.
```
:::

::: {.notes}
DEMO INSTRUCTIONS: Switch to the XYZ Corp Data Simulation. Type the prompt above (or similar). As results load, narrate what is happening: "Watch --- it is querying Salesforce first, then the Legacy CRM, then HubSpot. Three separate queries. Now it is using Python to merge the results with fuzzy name matching."

Let the full result appear. Point out key elements:
- The table showing multi-division customers with combined revenue
- The name mismatches (GE vs. General Electric, CAT vs. Caterpillar)
- The summary stats: 10 customers across multiple divisions, 4 buying from all 3

Say: "That took about 30 seconds. The same question that took the BI team 3 weeks. Same data. Same systems. Different interface."

If the live demo fails, switch to the pre-recorded fallback and narrate over it.
:::

---

## Demo Result: Cross-System Merge {.shrink}

::: {.comparison-table}

| Customer | Industrial (SF) | Energy (Legacy) | Safety (HubSpot) | Combined Revenue | Divisions |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| General Electric | General Electric | GE Industrial Solutions | GE Safety Division | $18.4M | 3 |
| ExxonMobil | ExxonMobil Corp | Exxon Mobil Energy Svcs | ExxonMobil Safety | $14.7M | 3 |
| Dow Chemical | Dow Chemical Co. | Dow Energy Products | Dow Inc. | $12.1M | 3 |
| Chevron | Chevron USA Inc. | Chevron Energy | Chevron Phillips Safety | $11.3M | 3 |
| 3M Company | 3M Industrial | --- | 3M Safety Products | $8.6M | 2 |

:::

::: {.highlight-box}
**10 customers** buy from 2+ divisions. **4** buy from all 3 --- representing **$56.5M** (11.4% of total revenue). Only 3 of 10 had matching names across systems.
:::

::: {.notes}
Display this summary slide while the demo result is still visible in the Data Simulation. Say: "Here is what the agent found. Ten customers purchase from multiple divisions. Four of them --- GE, ExxonMobil, Dow, and Chevron --- buy from all three divisions, representing $56.5 million in combined revenue."

"But here is the insight that matters: only 3 of the 10 had matching names across systems. The other 7 required fuzzy matching to link. Without that matching, these customers appear as 24 separate accounts. You cannot negotiate enterprise pricing. You cannot see the full relationship. You cannot even answer the CEO's question."

Pause: "This is the problem that AI solves. Not just querying one database --- any dashboard can do that. Querying multiple systems and reconciling the messy reality of how the same customer appears differently in each one."

"Now, an important caveat: this works because the schema descriptions were pre-written and the data is clean. In your organization, expect 60 to 90 days of data cataloging before the first query. We will cover exactly what that involves later this session."
:::

---

## Behind the Scenes

::: {.step-flow}
::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
1
:::
::: {.step-title}
Query SF
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Pull customers + revenue from Salesforce CRM
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
2
:::
::: {.step-title}
Query Legacy
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Pull from Energy division's custom CRM
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
3
:::
::: {.step-title}
Query HubSpot
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Pull from Safety division's CRM
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
4
:::
::: {.step-title}
Fuzzy Merge
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Python matches names across systems (GE = General Electric)
:::
:::
:::

::: {.explainer}
The agent **cannot** join across systems in a single query. It pulls from each, then uses Python to reconcile. This mirrors how a real enterprise AI tool works.
:::

::: {.notes}
Walk through each step card. Say: "What you just saw was not a single database query. The agent queried three separate systems, one at a time. It pulled customer data and revenue from Salesforce, then the Legacy CRM, then HubSpot. Then it used Python to merge the results."

"The merge step is where the magic happens. The agent used fuzzy name matching to link 'General Electric' in Salesforce to 'GE Industrial Solutions' in the Legacy CRM to 'GE Safety Division' in HubSpot. An exact-match join would have missed 7 of the 10 multi-division customers."

"This is important to understand: the agent cannot join across systems in a single SQL query. It queries each system where the data lives, then reconciles in code. This mirrors how a real enterprise AI tool would work --- and it is different from a data warehouse, where you would move everything into one place first."
:::

---

## The Business Lens {.section-divider}

::: {.notes}
This transitions to the business implications segment (minutes 23-33). You are connecting what they just saw to the SaaSpocalypse from Session 1 and to the broader strategic implications.
:::

---

## Remember the SaaSpocalypse?

::: {.two-cards}
::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
What the Market Saw
:::
- AI agents querying enterprise data in plain English
- Instant charts and narratives from a chat interface
- Cross-system answers that used to require BI teams
- [$2 trillion in software market cap erased]{.amber}
:::

::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
What You Just Saw
:::
- The XYZ Corp Data Simulation answered in 30 seconds what took 3 weeks
- Fuzzy matching across 3 CRMs with different naming conventions
- Combined revenue view that no single dashboard could produce
- [This is the technology behind the valuation collapse]{.accent}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "In Session 1, I told you about the SaaSpocalypse --- AI agents wiped $2 trillion from software company valuations because the market believes this technology will displace dashboards, reporting teams, and data integration tools."

"What you just saw is the technology behind that belief. An AI agent querying enterprise data in plain English, merging results across systems that do not talk to each other, and delivering an instant answer with charts and narrative."

"Now, whether the market is right about the magnitude --- that is debatable. But the capability is real. You just used it."

Ask the room: "If anyone in your company could do what you just saw --- ask a question in English and get an instant answer from your enterprise systems --- what changes? What roles are affected? What processes become unnecessary?"

Let 2-3 people respond. This is a rich discussion moment.
:::

---

## The Implication

::: {.three-cards}
::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
Dashboards
:::
- Pre-built views of pre-selected metrics
- Cannot answer questions they were not designed for
- Every new question requires a development cycle
:::

::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
BI Teams
:::
- Skilled analysts writing SQL and building reports
- Bottleneck: 3-week turnaround for cross-system questions
- High value, but limited bandwidth
:::

::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
AI Agents
:::
- Any question, any time, in plain English
- Cross-system queries with automatic reconciliation
- [Not a replacement --- a force multiplier]{.amber}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Walk through each card. Say: "Dashboards are great for monitoring known metrics. But they cannot answer questions they were not designed for. Every new question requires a development cycle."

"BI teams are highly skilled. But they are a bottleneck. The 3-week email chain is not because anyone is bad at their job --- it is because there are too many questions and too few analysts."

"AI agents sit in a different category. Any question, any time, in plain English. But I want to be clear: this is not a replacement for dashboards or BI teams. It is a force multiplier. Dashboards still monitor KPIs. BI teams still build validated data models. The agent handles the ad hoc questions that today go unanswered or take weeks."

Transition: "Now it is your turn. Let me get you logged into the XYZ Corp Data Simulation."
:::

---

## Hands-On: Your First Enterprise Queries {.section-divider}

::: {.notes}
This opens the hands-on segment (minutes 33-53). This is the longest segment of the session --- 20 minutes. Students will log into the XYZ Corp Data Simulation and work through guided prompts, progressing from single-system to cross-system queries. Make sure login credentials are ready to share.
:::

---

## Getting Started

::: {.info-box}
::: {.box-title}
Log In Now
:::
1. Open your browser and navigate to the XYZ Corp Data Simulation URL (in chat)
2. Log in with your provided credentials
3. You should see a chat interface --- type your first question
:::

::: {.explainer}
Start with single-system queries to get comfortable. Then we will push into cross-system territory.
:::

::: {.notes}
Share the URL and credentials via Zoom chat. Give students 2 minutes to log in. Troubleshoot any access issues. Once most people are in, say: "Great. You should see a chat interface. This is the same tool I just demoed. You are going to work through a series of guided queries, starting simple and getting more complex."

"Start with single-system queries to get comfortable. These hit one database at a time. Then we will push into cross-system territory, where the agent has to query multiple systems and merge results."
:::

---

## Round 1: Single-System Queries

::: {.three-cards}
::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
Salesforce (Industrial CRM)
:::
- "What is the total pipeline value for the Industrial division?"
- "Who are the top 5 customers by revenue in 2025?"
:::

::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
NetSuite (Finance)
:::
- "What was total revenue by quarter for 2025?"
- "Show operating expenses by category for Q4"
:::

::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
Workday (HR)
:::
- "How many employees does each division have?"
- "What is the average tenure by department?"
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "Round 1 --- single-system queries. Each of these hits one database. Try at least two from different systems. I have suggested some prompts, but feel free to ask your own questions."

Give students about 5-6 minutes on this round. Circulate (watch Zoom chat for questions). Point out interesting results if you see them. The goal is to build comfort with the interface before moving to cross-system queries.

After 5-6 minutes: "Good. Now let us make it harder."
:::

---

## Round 2: Cross-System Queries

::: {.two-cards}
::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
Try These
:::
- "Which customers buy from multiple divisions?"
- "What is revenue per employee by division?"
- "Compare support ticket volume to revenue by division"
- "Which division's headcount grew fastest relative to revenue?"
:::

::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
Watch For
:::
- The agent querying multiple systems sequentially
- Python merge steps appearing in the response
- Fuzzy name matching across CRMs
- Narrative that synthesizes data from different sources
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "Round 2 --- cross-system queries. These require the agent to pull from multiple databases and merge the results. Watch what happens behind the scenes. You will see it query one system, then another, then use Python to combine the data."

"Try the suggested prompts, or ask your own cross-system questions. The more specific your prompt, the better the output."

Give students about 7-8 minutes. This is where the real "aha" moments happen.
:::

---

## Demo Result: Revenue per Employee {.shrink}

::: {.comparison-table}

| Division | 2025 Revenue | Headcount | Revenue / Employee |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy | $52.1M | 134 | **$388,800** |
| Industrial | $87.3M | 218 | **$400,500** |
| Safety | $34.8M | 87 | **$400,000** |
| **Company-wide** | **$174.2M** | **482** | **$361,400** |

:::

::: {.highlight-box}
Company-wide average is **10% below** divisional averages because 43 Corporate HQ employees generate no direct revenue. All divisions cluster tightly around $390K-$401K per employee.
:::

::: {.notes}
Use this slide if students need a reference point, or to discuss the cross-system revenue-per-employee result. Say: "Here is what the revenue-per-employee query returns. Notice that this required data from two different types of systems --- CRM data for revenue and Workday for headcount. No single system has both."

"The interesting finding: all three divisions cluster tightly around $390K to $401K per employee. The company-wide average is lower because 43 corporate employees generate no direct revenue. That context matters --- without it, you might conclude the company is underperforming."
:::

---

## Timed Challenge {.centered-statement}

## 5 Minutes

::: {.highlight-box}
"Which division's top customer represents the greatest concentration risk --- and what does their support ticket history look like?"
:::

::: {.explainer}
This requires CRM data (customer revenue) + Zendesk data (support tickets). Go.
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "Timed challenge. You have 5 minutes. The question is on screen: which division's top customer represents the greatest concentration risk, and what does their support ticket history look like?"

"This requires data from at least two systems --- CRM for customer revenue concentration, and Zendesk for support tickets. Ask the XYZ Corp Data Simulation and see what you get."

Start a visible timer. After 5 minutes, ask: "Who got an answer? What did you find?" The expected answer is that Energy's top customer (CAT Energy Services) represents 34% of revenue --- a significant concentration risk. If students also surface the support ticket data, even better.

Use this as a teaching moment about the value of cross-system questions.
:::

---

## What We're Skipping {.section-divider}

::: {.notes}
This opens the expectations calibration segment (minutes 53-58). You are naming the pre-work required before any agent can query real systems. This is critical for preventing the "we can do this on Monday" reaction. Compressed to 5 minutes --- hit the key points and move on.
:::

---

## The 60-90 Days the Demo Didn't Show {.shrink}

::: {.four-cards}
::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
Data Cataloging
:::
- Inventory every table, column, and relationship
- Document what each field actually means
- Map data quality issues
:::

::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
Schema Documentation
:::
- Write descriptions the LLM can understand
- The system prompt is "institutional knowledge"
- Gets it wrong? Fix the schema description
:::

::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
API Access
:::
- Provision read-only database connections
- Set up authentication and rate limits
- Security review for each system
:::

::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
PII Classification
:::
- Identify sensitive fields (salary, SSN, health data)
- Define access control rules per role
- Decide what the LLM is allowed to see
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "Before we move to the next demo, I need to calibrate expectations. Everything you have seen today was impressive. But the XYZ Corp Data Simulation demo hides something important: the 60 to 90 days of pre-work that happened before the first question was ever asked."

Walk through each card quickly: "Someone had to catalog every table and column. Someone had to write schema descriptions the LLM can understand. Someone had to provision API access with proper security. And someone had to classify every field for PII sensitivity."

"This pre-work is real. It is not glamorous. And it is the difference between a demo and a production deployment. I name it now so that when you go back to your organizations, you also know what it takes to get there."

"We will dig deeper into deployment architecture and data security in Session 5. Now --- let me show you something different. Not all company knowledge lives in databases."

Transition: advance to the document AI demo.
:::

---

## Not All Knowledge Lives in Databases {.section-divider}

::: {.notes}
This opens the document AI / RAG introduction (minutes 58-68). You are pivoting from structured data (databases) to unstructured data (documents). This is a natural extension of the "scattered data" theme --- the same company that has 9 database systems also has hundreds of policies, contracts, and procedures trapped in PDFs and SharePoint folders.
:::

---

## The Other Half of Enterprise Knowledge

::: {.explainer}
You have seen AI query **databases** --- rows, columns, SQL. But think about where the bulk of your company's knowledge actually lives.
:::

::: {.stat-cards}
::: {.stat-card}
::: {.stat-number}
80%
:::
::: {.stat-label}
of enterprise data is unstructured (IDC) --- policies, contracts, emails, reports
:::
:::

::: {.stat-card}
::: {.stat-number}
0%
:::
::: {.stat-label}
of that is accessible through a dashboard
:::
:::

::: {.stat-card}
::: {.stat-number}
?
:::
::: {.stat-label}
How many critical answers are trapped in PDFs nobody can search?
:::
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "Everything we have done so far has been structured data --- rows, columns, SQL queries. But think about where the bulk of your company's knowledge actually lives. It is not in databases. It is in policies nobody has read since onboarding. Contracts buried in a shared drive. Board minutes from last quarter. Procedures documented in PDFs that SharePoint's search function cannot meaningfully query."

"Industry analysts at IDC estimate 80% of enterprise data is unstructured. Zero percent of that shows up in your dashboards. AI can search these documents too --- and it can do something a keyword search cannot: understand what you are asking and synthesize answers across multiple documents."

"Let me show you what that looks like."
:::

---

## Demo: Document-Based Q&A {.shrink}

::: {.code-box}
::: {.box-title}
Prompt 1 --- Policy Lookup
:::
```
What is our policy on remote work for the Energy Division?
```
:::

::: {.notes}
DEMO INSTRUCTIONS: Switch to the XYZ Corp Data Simulation's document collection. Type the prompt above. As the result loads, narrate: "Watch --- it is searching the document corpus, not a database. It is looking for passages relevant to remote work policy."

Let the full result appear. Point out key elements:
- The answer cites specific documents, sections, and page numbers
- The agent found a division-specific override in the Energy Division policies that differs from the general handbook
- It synthesized across two sources to give a complete answer

Say: "Notice the citations. Every claim traces to a specific document, section, and page number. This is what makes document AI usable in a corporate setting --- the user can verify. It is not just giving you an answer. It is showing you where that answer came from."

"Also notice: the Energy Division has a specific override to the general remote work policy. The agent found both the general policy and the override, and synthesized them. A keyword search for 'remote work' might have found one; the AI found and connected both."
:::

---

## Demo Result: Policy Lookup with Citations

::: {.two-cards}
::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
What the Agent Found
:::
- **General policy** (Employee Handbook, Section 4.2, p. 12): Up to 2 days/week remote for eligible roles
- **Energy Division override** (Energy Division Policies, Section 2.1, p. 3): Field operations staff exempt; office staff limited to 1 day/week during Q4 operational reviews
- Agent synthesized both sources into a complete answer
:::

::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
Why This Matters
:::
- [Every claim has a citation]{.accent} --- document, section, page number
- Cross-document reasoning: two sources, one answer
- A keyword search would likely find one source, not both
- The user can verify every claim against the original documents
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "Here is the result. The agent found the general remote work policy in the Employee Handbook and a division-specific override in the Energy Division Policies. It synthesized both into one answer with full citations."

"This is the first key point about document AI: citations matter. In a corporate setting, you need to know where an answer came from so you can verify it. The agent is not just retrieving --- it is citing."

"The second key point: cross-document reasoning. The question required connecting information from two different documents. A keyword search in SharePoint might have found the general policy. The AI found both the general policy and the exception, and told you how they interact."
:::

---

## Demo: Structured Data Extraction {.shrink}

::: {.code-box}
::: {.box-title}
Prompt 2 --- Vendor Renewals
:::
```
Which vendor contracts are up for renewal in the next 6 months?
What are the key terms and required notice periods?
```
:::

::: {.notes}
DEMO INSTRUCTIONS: Type the second prompt. As results load, narrate: "This is a different kind of document query. Instead of a policy question, we are asking for structured information trapped inside contract documents."

Point out:
- The agent extracted tabular data from a vendor registry document
- It pulled renewal dates, notice periods, and auto-renewal clauses
- It flagged a procedural requirement from a separate policy document (120-day review requirement)
- It noted that one contract had already auto-renewed because the review window was missed

Say: "This is structured data extraction from unstructured documents. The contract terms exist in PDFs and Word documents --- not in a database. The agent pulled them out, organized them into a table, and added procedural context from a separate policy."

"Notice it flagged that one contract already auto-renewed because the 120-day review window passed. That is the kind of insight that gets lost when policies live in unread PDFs."
:::

---

## Demo: Combining Documents + Database {.shrink}

::: {.code-box}
::: {.box-title}
Prompt 3 --- Cross-System Synthesis
:::
```
What's our contractual commitment to GE, and how does their
actual spending compare?
```
:::

::: {.notes}
DEMO INSTRUCTIONS: This is the most powerful demo of the session. Type the prompt. Narrate: "Watch what happens here. This question cannot be answered by either the document collection or the database alone. The agent needs both."

As results load: "It is retrieving the GE Master Service Agreement from the document collection for the contractual commitment. Now it is querying the CRM databases for actual spending across divisions."

Let the result appear. Point out:
- The contract says $4.2M minimum annual commitment with discount tiers and a shortfall clause
- The database shows actual spending: $4.28M in 2024 (just barely met), $3.80M in 2025 (below commitment)
- The agent calculated a $404K shortfall with a renewal deadline 92 days away
- The insight only emerges when both sources are combined

Say: "Neither source alone answers this question. The contract says what we promised. The database says what actually happened. The insight --- a $404K shortfall with a renewal deadline 92 days away --- only emerges when both are combined."

"This is the real value of enterprise AI. Most valuable business questions span structured data and unstructured documents. An AI that can only do one is half-useful."
:::

---

## Demo Result: Documents + Database Combined

::: {.comparison-table}

| Capability | Source | What it provided |
|---|---|---|
| Document AI (RAG) | GE Master Service Agreement | $4.2M minimum commitment, discount tiers, shortfall clause, renewal terms |
| Database AI (SQL) | Salesforce + Legacy CRM + HubSpot | Actual spending by division and year: $4.28M (2024), $3.80M (2025) |

:::

::: {.highlight-box}
Neither source alone answers the question. The contract says what we [promised]{.accent}. The database says what actually [happened]{.amber}. The insight --- a **$404K shortfall** with a renewal deadline **92 days away** --- only emerges when both are combined.
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "This slide summarizes what you just saw. Document AI retrieved the contract terms. Database AI retrieved the spending data. The agent combined them and calculated the gap."

"Cross-system identity resolution still matters here. GE appears as 'General Electric' in Salesforce, 'GE Industrial Solutions' in the contract, and 'GE Safety Division' in HubSpot. The agent had to reconcile all of these."

"The output was not just retrieval --- it calculated the gap, identified the penalty exposure, flagged the declining trend, and noted the 90-day renewal window. This is what separates useful AI from a search engine."

"A decision-maker can verify both sides independently. The response cites the specific MSA section for the commitment and identifies which CRM system produced each spending figure."
:::

---

## How RAG Works {.shrink}

::: {.step-flow-5}
::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
1
:::
::: {.step-title}
Chunk
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Documents split into passages (paragraphs, sections, or clauses)
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
2
:::
::: {.step-title}
Embed
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Each chunk converted to a numerical vector by an embedding model
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
3
:::
::: {.step-title}
Store
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Vectors stored in a vector database for fast similarity search
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
4
:::
::: {.step-title}
Query
:::
::: {.step-desc}
User's question is embedded and matched against stored chunks
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
5
:::
::: {.step-title}
Generate
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Top matches sent to LLM as context; LLM answers with citations
:::
:::
:::

::: {.explainer}
**RAG** = Retrieval-Augmented Generation. The LLM does not memorize your documents --- it [searches them at query time]{.accent} and answers using only what it retrieves.
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "Let me pull back the curtain on how that document Q&A actually works. The technique is called RAG --- Retrieval-Augmented Generation. Here is the pipeline in five steps."

Walk through each step: "First, your documents are split into chunks --- paragraphs, sections, or clauses. Second, each chunk is converted into a numerical vector by an embedding model --- essentially, a mathematical fingerprint of the text's meaning. Third, those vectors are stored in a vector database optimized for similarity search."

"When a user asks a question, their question is embedded the same way and matched against stored chunks --- step four. The closest matches are retrieved. Step five: those matches are sent to the LLM as context, and the LLM generates an answer using only the retrieved passages, with citations back to the originals."

"The key concept here is chunking --- how you split your documents determines what the AI can and cannot find. Chunk too large and retrieval is noisy. Chunk too small and you lose context. We will see a chunking failure in the Atherton case you will read before Session 6 --- a $1.5 million contract clause was split across two chunks, and the second half fell below the retrieval threshold. The agent returned a confident, well-cited answer that was missing a critical penalty. That is the kind of failure you need to know about."

"For now, the essential takeaway: the LLM does not memorize your documents. It searches them at query time and answers using only what it retrieves."

"One more thing to keep in mind: RAG is not infallible. Long passages split across chunks can lose meaning. Retrieved passages can be taken out of context. And if source documents are outdated, the answers will be too. We will examine a real-world case of these failures --- the Atherton Financial case --- later in the course."
:::

---

## Breakout: How Scattered Is Your Data? {.section-divider}

::: {.notes}
This opens the breakout discussion (minutes 73-82). Groups of 3-4 compare their data landscapes. The key difference from earlier versions: this breakout now includes both database systems AND document collections. Assign breakout rooms now.
:::

---

## Breakout Discussion (9 minutes)

::: {.two-cards}
::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
Map Your Landscape
:::
- **3-5 core systems** (CRM, ERP, HR, finance, support)
- **2-3 document collections** that trap important knowledge (policies, contracts, procedures, board materials)
- Where is integration hardest?
- What cross-system questions would have the most business value?
:::

::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
Report Back
:::
- **One common pattern** your group identified
- **One surprising idiosyncrasy** unique to someone's company
- Where does the most valuable knowledge live --- [databases or documents]{.accent}?
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "Breakout time. Groups of 3 to 4. I want you to map your company's data landscape --- and this time, include both systems and documents."

"List your 3 to 5 core database systems --- not categories, but actual system names. Salesforce. SAP. Workday. Then add 2 to 3 document collections that trap important knowledge. The contracts folder on SharePoint. The compliance policies in a shared drive. The board materials that only executives can access."

"Compare: what do you have in common? Where is integration hardest? What cross-system questions would be most valuable? And here is a new question based on what you just saw: where does the most valuable knowledge live --- in your databases or in your documents?"

"When we come back, each group reports: one common pattern and one surprising idiosyncrasy. You have 9 minutes."

Send students to breakout rooms. After 7 minutes, broadcast a 2-minute warning. When groups return, take 3-4 minutes for report-backs. Common patterns: everyone has a CRM-to-finance gap; HR data is always siloed; contract terms are trapped in PDFs; customer naming is inconsistent everywhere. The document collection insight is new --- many students will realize their most critical knowledge is not in databases at all.

This data map carries forward --- it becomes the foundation for the capstone strategy in Session 8.
:::

---

## Bridge to Session 4 {.section-divider}

::: {.notes}
This is the bridge to the next session (minutes 82-87). You are previewing the transition from asking questions to producing complete deliverables.
:::

---

## What Comes Next

::: {.two-cards}
::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
Today: Asking Questions
:::
- Single-system queries to databases
- Cross-system queries with fuzzy matching
- Document Q&A with citations
- Combined document + database queries
:::

::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
Session 4: Producing Deliverables
:::
- Data → charts → narrative → board-ready memo
- The full reporting pipeline in a single prompt
- Automated weekly reports and executive summaries
- [The agent becomes your reporting pipeline]{.amber}
:::
:::

::: {.explainer}
You have seen AI **answer questions**. Next session: the same agent **produces complete deliverables** --- memos, reports, risk assessments. The difference between a chat reply and a board presentation.
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "Let me preview what comes next. Today was about asking questions --- querying databases, searching documents, combining both. Next session, we go from asking questions to producing complete deliverables."

"Instead of getting a number or a chart, you will get a board-ready memo. Instead of a table of results, you will get an executive summary with traffic-light formatting. The same agent that answers questions can produce the entire reporting pipeline --- from raw data to finished document."

"We will also look at how these pipelines can run on schedules --- automated Monday morning dashboards, monthly board decks, weekly sales forecasts. And we will see a real-world example: HPE's CFO used an AI agent to replace a 100-slide weekly performance review."

"But that is Wednesday. For now --- homework."
:::

---

## Homework {.section-divider}

::: {.notes}
Wrap-up segment (minutes 87-90). Assign homework and close.
:::

---

## This Week

::: {.info-box}
::: {.box-title}
Homework for Session 4
:::
1. **Log into the XYZ Corp Data Simulation** and ask **10 business questions** --- at least 3 cross-system and at least 2 from the document collection
2. **Write down** one question the agent answered well and one where it was unsatisfying or wrong --- bring both to Session 4
3. **Complete your data map:** at least 5 systems your company uses, the top 3 cross-system questions you cannot answer today, and 2-3 document collections that trap important knowledge
4. *Optional viewing:* Demos 6-7 show building a RAG system from scratch and combining it with a database agent --- deeper context for the document Q&A introduced today
:::

::: {.highlight-box}
**Next session:** We go from asking questions to producing **complete deliverables** --- memos, reports, risk assessments. The agent becomes your reporting pipeline.
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "Homework. First, log into the XYZ Corp Data Simulation this week and ask at least 10 business questions. Push it. Ask at least 3 cross-system questions and at least 2 from the document collection. Write down your best answer and your worst answer --- we will use both in Session 4."

"Second, complete your data map. List at least 5 systems your company uses, the top 3 cross-system questions you cannot answer today, and 2 to 3 document collections that trap important knowledge. This carries forward to your capstone."

"If you want to go deeper on how the document Q&A works, Demos 6 and 7 on the course site show how to build a RAG system from scratch and combine it with a database agent. These are optional but give you a behind-the-scenes look at what you used today."

"Next session, we go from asking questions to producing complete deliverables. Instead of getting a number or a chart, you will get a board-ready memo. See you Wednesday."
:::

---

## Session 3 Summary {.centered-statement}

## AI Meets Your Data

::: {.stat-cards}
::: {.stat-card}
::: {.stat-number}
30 sec
:::
::: {.stat-label}
vs. 3 weeks for a cross-system question
:::
:::

::: {.stat-card}
::: {.stat-number}
80%
:::
::: {.stat-label}
of enterprise data is unstructured (IDC) --- policies, contracts, documents
:::
:::

::: {.stat-card}
::: {.stat-number}
2 paths
:::
::: {.stat-label}
Database AI + Document AI = the full picture
:::
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
This is a recap slide to display as students leave. You do not need to present it unless you want a quick close. The key takeaways are visual: the speed difference for structured data, the 80% of enterprise knowledge trapped in documents, and the power of combining both retrieval paths.
:::
